title: How a Volcanic Eruption Helped Unleash the Black Death in Europe in 1347
description:
in History, Science |   	May 8th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The flap of a but┬Łter┬Łfly┬ŁŌĆÖs wings on one side of the world can cause a hur┬Łri┬Łcan
body:
in History, Science |   	May 8th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The flap of a but┬Łter┬Łfly┬ŁŌĆÖs wings on one side of the world can cause a hur┬Łri┬Łcane on the oth┬Łer, or so they say. If we take it a bit too lit┬Łer┬Łal┬Łly, that old obser┬Łva┬Łtion may make us won┬Łder what a hur┬Łri┬Łcane can cause. Or if not a hur┬Łri┬Łcane, how about anoth┬Łer kind of large-scale nat┬Łur┬Łal dis┬Łas┬Łter? If new find┬Łings by researchers from the Uni┬Łver┬Łsi┬Łty of Cam┬Łbridge and the Leib┬Łniz Insti┬Łtute for the His┬Łto┬Łry and Cul┬Łture of East┬Łern Europe are to be believed, a vol┬ŁcanoŌĆÖs erup┬Łtion helped lead to the out┬Łbreak and spread of the Black Death across Europe in the four┬Łteenth cen┬Łtu┬Łry. In the video above, British his┬Łto┬Łry and envi┬Łron┬Łmen┬Łtal sci┬Łence spe┬Łcial┬Łist Paul Whitewick explains the evi┬Łdence on a vis┬Łit to one of the aban┬Łdoned medieval vil┬Łlages strick┬Łen by that plague.
As Cam┬ŁbridgeŌĆÖs Sarah Collins writes, ŌĆ£the evi┬Łdence sug┬Łgests that a vol┬Łcanic erup┬Łtion ŌĆö or clus┬Łter of erup┬Łtions ŌĆö around 1345 caused annu┬Łal tem┬Łper┬Ła┬Łtures to drop for con┬Łsec┬Łu┬Łtive years due to the haze from vol┬Łcanic ash and gas┬Łes, which in turn caused crops to fail across the Mediter┬Łranean region.ŌĆØ Des┬Łper┬Łate Ital┬Łian city-states thus fell back on trad┬Łing with grain pro┬Łduc┬Łers around the Black Sea. ŌĆ£This cli┬Łmate-dri┬Łven change in long-dis┬Łtance trade routes helped avoid famine, but in addi┬Łtion to life-sav┬Łing food, the ships were car┬Łry┬Łing the dead┬Łly bac┬Łteri┬Łum that ulti┬Łmate┬Łly caused the Black Death, enabling the first and dead┬Łliest wave of the sec┬Łond plague pan┬Łdem┬Łic to gain a foothold in Europe.ŌĆØ
An impor┬Łtant clue came in the form of ŌĆ£infor┬Łma┬Łtion con┬Łtained in tree rings from the Span┬Łish Pyre┬Łnees, where con┬Łsec┬Łu┬Łtive ŌĆśBlue RingsŌĆÖ point to unusu┬Łal┬Łly cold and wet sum┬Łmers in 1345, 1346 and 1347 across much of south┬Łern Europe.ŌĆØ Records of lunar eclipses and lay┬Łers of sul┬Łfur locked into ice cores dat┬Łing to about the same time fur┬Łther height┬Łen the prob┬Ła┬Łbil┬Łi┬Łty of vol┬Łcanic activ┬Łi┬Łty. Key to tying these dis┬Łparate pieces of evi┬Łdence togeth┬Łer are changes in trade routes: on a map, Whitewick traces ŌĆ£move┬Łment increas┬Łing along these cor┬Łri┬Łdors, grain imports to the mar┬Łitime republics of Venice and Genoa from north of the Black Sea and beyond, in 1347.ŌĆØ Accord┬Łing to writ┬Łten records, the Black Death came to Britain the fol┬Łlow┬Łing year, arriv┬Łing in ŌĆ£a coun┬Łtry already shaped by failed har┬Łvests, weak┬Łened com┬Łmu┬Łni┬Łties, and ris┬Łing move┬Łment of peo┬Łple and goods.ŌĆØ
Some com┬Łmu┬Łni┬Łties weath┬Łered the plague and, in the full┬Łness of time, even bounced back; oth┬Łers, like the vil┬Łlage amid whose remains Whitewick stands, prac┬Łti┬Łcal┬Łly van┬Łished alto┬Łgeth┬Łer. ŌĆ£This was a glob┬Łal prob┬Łlem that became very much a local one,ŌĆØ he says, under┬Łscor┬Łing its rev┬Łe┬Łla┬Łtion of the risk fac┬Łtors present even in the ear┬Łly stages of what we now call glob┬Łal┬Łiza┬Łtion. ŌĆ£A vol┬Łcanic erup┬Łtion thou┬Łsands of miles away altered cli┬Łmate pat┬Łterns, and that cli┬Łmate reshaped har┬Łvest and trade, and trade car┬Łried dis┬Łease. And here, in the qui┬Łet Eng┬Łlish fields, the con┬Łse┬Łquences have set┬Łtled into the ground:ŌĆØ not quite as poet┬Łic an image as the but┬Łter┬Łfly and the hur┬Łri┬Łcane, grant┬Łed, but hard┬Łly less rel┬Łe┬Łvant to our own world for it.
Relat┬Łed Con┬Łtent:
The His┬Łto┬Łry of the Plague: Every Major Epi┬Łdem┬Łic in an Ani┬Łmat┬Łed Map
A 1665 Adver┬Łtise┬Łment Promis┬Łes a ŌĆ£Famous and Effec┬Łtu┬ŁalŌĆØ Cure for the Great Plague
The Strange Cos┬Łtumes of the Plague Doc┬Łtors Who Treat┬Łed 17th Cen┬Łtu┬Łry Vic┬Łtims of the Bubon┬Łic Plague
How the Sur┬Łvivors of Pom┬Łpeii Escaped Mount Vesu┬ŁviusŌĆÖ Dead┬Łly Erup┬Łtion: A TED-Ed Ani┬Łma┬Łtion Tells the Sto┬Łry
The 1883 Kraka┬Łtoa Explo┬Łsion Made the Loud┬Łest Sound in His┬Łto┬Łry ŌĆö So Loud It Trav┬Łeled Around the World Four Times
1,000 Years of Medieval Euro┬Łpean His┬Łto┬Łry in 20 Min┬Łutes
Based in Seoul,┬ĀCol┬Łin┬ĀMarshall┬Āwrites and broad┬Łcasts on cities, lan┬Łguage, and cul┬Łture. HeŌĆÖs the author of the newslet┬Łter┬ĀBooks on Cities┬Āas well as the books┬ĀĒĢ£ĻĄŁ ņÜöņĢĮ ĻĖłņ¦Ć┬Ā(No Sum┬Łma┬Łriz┬Łing Korea) and┬ĀKore┬Łan Newtro.┬ĀFol┬Łlow him on the social net┬Łwork for┬Łmer┬Łly known as Twit┬Łter at┬Ā@colinmarshall.

title: Hilary Pecis Paints Saturated Snapshots of West Coast Life
description: In Love Letters, Hilary Pecis captures the mundane moments and under-appreciated views of daily life. The Los Angeles-based artist presents a suit
body:
In Love Letters, Hilary Pecis captures the mundane moments and under-appreciated views of daily life. The Los Angeles-based artist presents a suite of new acrylic paintings in her signature saturated style, focusing on snippets of a backyard pool, the corner of a studio worktop, and a friendly picnic complete with a radiant strawberry cake. 
Pecis prefers to work from photos and translates singular moments onto linen. Utilizing a uniform opacity in her paints, she incorporates both comparable and exaggerated colors and affords particular attention to texture and pattern. Frilly fronds on a plant, light radiating off the waterŌĆÖs surface, and the rough texture of a woven tablecloth each evidence the artistŌĆÖs meticulous process.
Love Letters opens at David Kordansky Gallery in Los Angeles on May 16 and runs through June 20. Until then, explore more of PecisŌĆÖ work on Instagram.
ŌĆ£PoolŌĆØ (2026), acrylic on linen, 92 x 77 x 1 5/8 inches
ŌĆ£Studio TulipsŌĆØ (2026), acrylic on linen, 44 x 34 x 1 1/2 inches
ŌĆ£Mt. ShastaŌĆØ (2025), acrylic on linen, 74 x 64 x 1 1/2 inches
ŌĆ£PicnicŌĆØ (2026), acrylic on linen, 92 x 77 x 1 5/8 inches
ŌĆ£MedalsŌĆØ (2026), acrylic on linen, 77 x 92 x 1 5/8 inches

title: In 'Reverence,' Three Decades of Paul Nicklen's Remarkable Photographs Exalt Nature
description: Known for his stunning photos of wildlife and landscapes, as well as co-founding SeaLegacy alongside fellow conservationist and photographer Crist
body:
Known for his stunning photos of wildlife and landscapes, as well as co-founding SeaLegacy alongside fellow conservationist and photographer Cristina Mittermeier, Paul Nicklen has traveled the globe to not only highlight our planetŌĆÖs phenomenal biodiversity but also to shed light on its increasing vulnerabilities due to the ongoing climate crisis.
NicklenŌĆÖs most ambitious project yet gathers myriad images from a career exploring the corners of the earth for more than three decades. Forthcoming from Hemeria, Reverence marks the most comprehensive collection of his work to date. The book features 160 photographs, including some of NicklenŌĆÖs most enduring images alongside others previously unpublished.

From the root-like system of the Colorado River delta to narwhals feeding on cod in the Arctic Bay off Baffin Island, NicklenŌĆÖs photos illuminate the vast and resilient beauty of the natural world. ŌĆ£Reverence is what we feel in the silent presence of a whale beneath the ice, in the fierce gaze of a polar bear, in the timeless dance of ocean and light,ŌĆØ says a statement. ŌĆ£It is what the natural world evokes when we stop long enough to truly see it.ŌĆØ
Reverence is slated for release on July 28, which is also World Conservation Day, and pre-orders are open now.

schema.headline: In 'Reverence,' Three Decades of Paul Nicklen's Remarkable Photographs Exalt Nature

title: Irina Werning Chronicles 18 Years of Photographing 'Las Pelilargas' in a New Book
description: For the better part of two decades, Irina Werning has traveled throughout Latin America searching for a specific trait: incredibly long hair. In h
body:
For the better part of two decades, Irina Werning has traveled throughout Latin America searching for a specific trait: incredibly long hair. In her photography series Las PelilargasŌĆömeaning ŌĆ£the long-haired onesŌĆØ in SpanishŌĆöshe chronicles a time-honored Indigenous tradition through a visual celebration of patience, joy, and cultural pride.
In a statement, Werning shares that when she asks young women in the many small towns sheŌĆÖs visited why they have long hair, they respond with simple reasons akin to, ŌĆ£Because I like it.ŌĆØ But, Werning adds, ŌĆ£The true reason is invisible and passes from generation to generation. ItŌĆÖs the culture of Latin America, where our ancestors believed that cutting hair was cutting life, that hair is the physical manifestation of our thoughts and our souls and our connection to the land.ŌĆØ

Nearly 90 images are included in WerningŌĆÖs new book, Las Pelilargas, published by GOST Books. The photos span 18 years, starting with the artistŌĆÖs first encounter with long-haired women in 2006 in Argentina, when she was photographing members of the Indigenous Kolla community. 
ŌĆ£Guided by her intuition, she went on to spend months in remote mountain towns putting up signs in schools, hospitals, and markets, and organising hair competitions in an effort to seek out those with long hair,ŌĆØ GOST says. Werning continued to make the portraits until 2024.┬ĀŌĆ£She found that traditions were not just surviving, but evolving┬Āwith long hair symbolising both┬Ācontinuity and subtle rebellion.ŌĆØ
Find your copy on Bookshop. You might also enjoy Celia D. LunaŌĆÖs series, Cholitas Bravas.

schema.headline: Irina Werning Chronicles 18 Years of Photographing 'Las Pelilargas' in a New Book

title: Explore 25 Incredible Photos of the Milky Way Captured Around the World
description: Organized by Capture the Atlas, the 2026 Milky Way Photographer of the Year saw a record number of submissions, with more than 6,500 entries repre
body:
Organized by Capture the Atlas, the 2026 Milky Way Photographer of the Year saw a record number of submissions, with more than 6,500 entries representing a wide range of landscapes and perspectives around the world. Just 25 were selected as the top images, representing 12 different regions from the Canary Islands to New Zealand to Argentina.
ŌĆ£Every year, this collection reminds us that photographing the Milky Way is not only about technique or planning. It is about curiosity, patience, and the desire to experience the night sky in places where it still feels wild,ŌĆØ says Dan Zafra, editor of Capture the Atlas and curator of the annual contest. ŌĆ£Many of these skies are becoming increasingly rare, and we hope these images inspire people not only to admire them, but also to value and protect them.ŌĆØ
Brendan Larsen, ŌĆ£Milky Way over Syme Hut and Mt. Taranaki.ŌĆØ Syme Hut, Mt Taranaki, New ZealandŌĆŗ
Max Terwindt, ŌĆ£Caldera Galaxy Panorama.ŌĆØ La Palma, Canary Islands
Lukasz Remkowicz, ŌĆ£Milky Way over the Tatra Mountains.ŌĆØ Jurg├│w, Poland
Leonel Padron, ŌĆ£Lost in the Ripples of Space and Time.ŌĆØ Pinnacles Desert, Nambung, Western Australia
Anastasia Gulova, ŌĆ£Galaxy on the Rise.ŌĆØ Tenerife, Canary Islands, Spain
Daniel Vi├▒├® Garcia, ŌĆ£My Perfect Night.ŌĆØ Catamarca, Argentina
Anthony Lopez, ŌĆ£Celestial Light over Sea Cliffs.ŌĆØ Saint Rapha├½l, French Riviera
Uro┼Ī Fink, ŌĆ£Geminid Symphony Over La PalmaŌĆÖs Guardian of the Sky.ŌĆØ Roque de los Muchachos GTC Telescope, La PalmaŌĆŗ

title: Jake Messing's Hyperrealistic Paintings Celebrate the Abundance of Nature
description: ŌĆ£The world hums with beauty and danger, harmony and discord,ŌĆØ says Jake Messing. ŌĆ£We walk through these shifting currents every day. For as long a
body:
ŌĆ£The world hums with beauty and danger, harmony and discord,ŌĆØ says Jake Messing. ŌĆ£We walk through these shifting currents every day. For as long as I can remember, I have turned toward the natural worldŌĆöstudying its patterns, its relationships, its quiet lessons.ŌĆØ
In highly detailed, hyperrealistic paintings, the Northern California-based artist explores nature as a reflection of our inner lives. Abundance and beauty are sometimes confronted with tension and discomfort, and through nature, ŌĆ£I question the fears and unspoken rules that shape us,ŌĆØ Messing says.
ŌĆ£Coccinellidaes Hideaway 2ŌĆØ
Working in acrylic on canvas, the artist composes otherworldly vignettes of flora and fauna, often uniting creatures and plants in situations weŌĆÖd be unlikely to encounter in the real world. Yet these dense, maximal clusters of succulents, insects, blossoms, birds, and other creatures summon what Messing describes as both ŌĆ£chaos and graceŌĆØ in a vibrant meditation on ecosystems,  interdependency, and biodiversity.
In an art historical sense, these works certainly nod to the meticulously detailed Dutch Golden Age oil paintings of the likes of Rachel Ruysch and Jan Brueghel the Elder, which were also typically set against deep backgrounds. Employing a bit of memento moriŌĆöa reminder of the inevitability of deathŌĆöthese often incorporated wilting petals and other nods to decay.
Messing taps into this tradition, yet he emphasizes full-blooded vivacity. Every floret and frond is bursting with life, while the occasional playful color gradient, bubbles, or shiny fabric place these compositions firmly in our time. ŌĆ£Through my work, I seek to bring the outside in, to honor the wildness that surrounds us, and to reveal the beauty and danger, the decay and renewal, that bind our outer and inner worlds together,ŌĆØ he says.
See more on the artistŌĆÖs Instagram.

title: Collaged Denim Sculptures by Nick Doyle Unravel American Mythology
description: Despite its name, the Canadian Tuxedo is a distinctly American look. The denim-on-denim getup dates back to the 1950s, when Bing Crosby sported a
body:
Despite its name, the Canadian Tuxedo is a distinctly American look. The denim-on-denim getup dates back to the 1950s, when Bing Crosby sported a full LeviŌĆÖs ensemble while in Vancouver, setting a sartorial trend that continues today.
The national mythology woven into this utilitarian material is also the focus of Brooklyn-based Nick Doyle, who layers denim atop denim into large wall sculptures. From a pair of aviators reflecting puffy clouds to a vast Rocky Mountain landscape framed by brick, the works evoke a sort of nostalgic road trip west, as if chasing a big break, and ultimately, realizing the American dream.
ŌĆ£First Come the DreamersŌĆØ (2026), bleached and collaged denim on panel, 25 x 72 inches
For Doyle, denim is a poignant, loaded metaphor for much of American culture and history. The material has roots in chattel slavery, when people enslaved in the South were dyeing cotton with indigo. ThereŌĆÖs also its association with the brusque masculinity of James Dean and cowboy ruggedness, itself an extension of the gold rush and Manifest Destiny. The fabric, in many ways, is a stand-in for the contradictions, hypocrisies, and unreachable desires so bound up in American life.
While researching the visual language of Americana in 2018, Doyle came upon a roll of denim discarded by a fashion designer moving out of his building. ŌĆ£At the time, I had no money, so I was making work out of material I┬Āfound┬Āin the garbage or at my local hardware store,ŌĆØ he shares. ŌĆ£As I was pulling [the roll] out of the trash, I noticed a network of ideas connecting in my brainŌĆ”┬ĀI felt the material reflected the historical complexities I was seeing in my research, as well as being reflected in my own familial history.ŌĆØ
This encounter was one of those providential moments that set off an enduring fascination. In his solo exhibition Collective Hallucinations, on view at Perrotin, Doyle presents the latest of his denim sculptures, including stylized cacti, landscapes cordoned off by chainlink fences, and more mystical objects like tarot cards and a life-sized fortune tellerŌĆÖs shop. 
ŌĆ£Innocent IndustryŌĆØ (2026), bleached and collaged denim on panel, 72 x 64 inches
The show contains myriad symbols of American exceptionalism and individualism, presented in the heritage fabric of the nation. Doyle shares:

Over the last few years, my conception of American mythology has only become more complexŌĆ” I think in a lot of ways what weŌĆÖre experiencing now is a breakdown of these mythologies.┬ĀThey are in direct conflict with the current political reality, yet they are summoned as if it is business as usual.┬ĀThe worldŌĆÖs image of America has changed, but our countryŌĆÖs nostalgia for itself is making us late to the party.┬ĀThereŌĆÖs┬Ātragedy in vanity.

Collective Hallucinations presents these unrealized dreams and confrontations in varying shades of blue, rendering what appears to be individual moments as simply different washes of the same story. 
In addition to his practice, Doyle will soon open a kink bar called Human Resources and is working toward a fall exhibition of paper collages and prints at Pace. If youŌĆÖre in New York, Collective Hallucinations runs through May 30. Otherwise, find more on Instagram.
ŌĆ£Here We Go Round the Prickly Pear BushŌĆØ (2026), bleached and collaged denim on panel, 48 x 26 inches
ŌĆ£The CloudsŌĆØ (2026), bleached denim on panel, 24 x 18 inches
ŌĆ£Plastic EdenŌĆØ (2026), bleached and collaged denim on panel, 68 x 42 inches
ŌĆ£Black Market BodiesŌĆØ (2026), bleached and collaged denim on panel, 36 x 64 inches

schema.headline: Collaged Denim Sculptures by Nick Doyle Unravel American Mythology
